NKJV.1: I tell the truth in Christ, I am not lying, my conscience also bearing me witness in the Holy Spirit,
NKJV.2: that I have great sorrow and continual grief in my heart.
NKJV.3: For I could wish that I myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren, my countrymen according to the flesh,
NKJV.4: who are Israelites, to whom pertain the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the giving of the law, the service of God, and the promises;
NKJV.5: of whom are the fathers and from whom, according to the flesh, Christ came, who is over all, the eternally blessed God. Amen.
NKJV.6: But it is not that the word of God has taken no effect. For they are not all Israel who are of Israel,
NKJV.7: nor are they all children because they are the seed of Abraham; but, “In Isaac your seed shall be called.”
NKJV.8: That is, those who are the children of the flesh, these are not the children of God; but the children of the promise are counted as the seed.
NKJV.9: For this is the word of promise: “At this time I will come and Sarah shall have a son.”
NKJV.10: And not only this, but when Rebecca also had conceived by one man, even by our father Isaac
NKJV.11: (for the children not yet being born, nor having done any good or evil, that the purpose of God according to election might stand, not of works but of Him who calls),
NKJV.12: it was said to her, “The older shall serve the younger.”
NKJV.13: As it is written, “Jacob I have loved, but Esau I have hated.”
NKJV.14: What shall we say then? Is there unrighteousness with God? Certainly not!
NKJV.15: For He says to Moses, “I will have mercy on whomever I will have mercy, and I will have compassion on whomever I will have compassion.”
NKJV.16: So then it is not of him who wills, nor of him who runs, but of God who shows mercy.
NKJV.17: For the Scripture says to the Pharaoh, “For this very purpose I have raised you up, that I may show My power in you, and that My name may be declared in all the earth.”
NKJV.18: Therefore He has mercy on whom He wills, and whom He wills He hardens.
NKJV.19: You will say to me then, “Why does He still find fault? For who has resisted His will?”
NKJV.20: But indeed, O man, who are you to reply against God? Will the thing formed say to him who formed it, “Why have you made me like this?”
NKJV.21: Does not the potter have power over the clay, from the same lump to make one vessel for honor and another for dishonor?
NKJV.22: What if God, wanting to show His wrath and to make His power known, endured with much longsuffering the vessels of wrath prepared for destruction,
NKJV.23: and that He might make known the riches of His glory on the vessels of mercy, which He had prepared beforehand for glory,
NKJV.24: even us whom He called, not of the Jews only, but also of the Gentiles?
NKJV.25: As He says also in Hosea:  “I will call them My people, who were not My people, And her beloved, who was not beloved.”
NKJV.26: “And it shall come to pass in the place where it was said to them, ‘You are not My people,’ There they shall be called sons of the living God.”
NKJV.27: Isaiah also cries out concerning Israel:  “Though the number of the children of Israel be as the sand of the sea,  The remnant will be saved.
NKJV.28: For He will finish the work and cut it short in righteousness, Because the LORD will make a short work upon the earth.”
NKJV.29: And as Isaiah said before: “Unless the LORD of Sabaoth had left us a seed, We would have become like Sodom, And we would have been made like Gomorrah.”
NKJV.30: What shall we say then? That Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness, have attained to righteousness, even the righteousness of faith;
NKJV.31: but Israel, pursuing the law of righteousness, has not attained to the law of righteousness.
NKJV.32: Why? Because they did not seek it by faith, but as it were, by the works of the law. For they stumbled at that stumbling stone.
NKJV.33: As it is written:  “Behold, I lay in Zion a stumbling stone and rock of offense, And whoever believes on Him will not be put to shame.”

ESV.1: I am speaking the truth in Christ — I am not lying; my conscience bears me witness in the Holy Spirit —
ESV.2: that I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart.
ESV.3: For I could wish that I myself were accursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my brothers,  my kinsmen according to the flesh.
ESV.4: They are Israelites, and to them belong the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the giving of the law, the worship, and the promises.
ESV.5: To them belong the patriarchs, and from their race, according to the flesh, is the Christ, who is God over all, blessed forever. Amen.
ESV.6: But it is not as though the word of God has failed. For not all who are descended from Israel belong to Israel,
ESV.7: and not all are children of Abraham because they are his offspring, but “Through Isaac shall your offspring be named.”
ESV.8: This means that it is not the children of the flesh who are the children of God, but the children of the promise are counted as offspring.
ESV.9: For this is what the promise said: “About this time next year I will return, and Sarah shall have a son.”
ESV.10: And not only so, but also when Rebekah had conceived children by one man, our forefather Isaac,
ESV.11: though they were not yet born and had done nothing either good or bad — in order that God's purpose of election might continue, not because of works but because of him who calls —
ESV.12: she was told, “The older will serve the younger.”
ESV.13: As it is written, “Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated.”
ESV.14: What shall we say then? Is there injustice on God's part? By no means!
ESV.15: For he says to Moses, “I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion.”
ESV.16: So then it depends not on human will or exertion,  but on God, who has mercy.
ESV.17: For the Scripture says to Pharaoh, “For this very purpose I have raised you up, that I might show my power in you, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth.”
ESV.18: So then he has mercy on whomever he wills, and he hardens whomever he wills.
ESV.19: You will say to me then, “Why does he still find fault? For who can resist his will?”
ESV.20: But who are you, O man, to answer back to God? Will what is molded say to its molder, “Why have you made me like this?”
ESV.21: Has the potter no right over the clay, to make out of the same lump one vessel for honorable use and another for dishonorable use?
ESV.22: What if God, desiring to show his wrath and to make known his power, has endured with much patience vessels of wrath prepared for destruction,
ESV.23: in order to make known the riches of his glory for vessels of mercy, which he has prepared beforehand for glory —
ESV.24: even us whom he has called, not from the Jews only but also from the Gentiles?
ESV.25: As indeed he says in Hosea, “Those who were not my people I will call ‘my people,’ and her who was not beloved I will call ‘beloved.’”
ESV.26: “And in the very place where it was said to them, ‘You are not my people,’ there they will be called ‘sons of the living God.’”
ESV.27: And Isaiah cries out concerning Israel: “Though the number of the sons of Israel  be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will be saved,
ESV.28: for the Lord will carry out his sentence upon the earth fully and without delay.”
ESV.29: And as Isaiah predicted,  “If the Lord of hosts had not left us offspring, we would have been like Sodom and become like Gomorrah.”
ESV.30: What shall we say, then? That Gentiles who did not pursue righteousness have attained it, that is, a righteousness that is by faith;
ESV.31: but that Israel who pursued a law that would lead to righteousness  did not succeed in reaching that law.
ESV.32: Why? Because they did not pursue it by faith, but as if it were based on works. They have stumbled over the stumbling stone,
ESV.33: as it is written, “Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offense; and whoever believes in him will not be put to shame.”

NASB.1: I am telling the truth in Christ, I am not lying, my conscience testifies with me in the Holy Spirit,
NASB.2: that I have great sorrow and unceasing grief in my heart.
NASB.3: For I could wish that I myself were accursed, [separated] from Christ for the sake of my brethren, my kinsmen according to the flesh,
NASB.4: who are Israelites, to whom belongs the adoption as sons, and the glory and the covenants and the giving of the Law and the [temple] service and the promises,
NASB.5: whose are the fathers, and from whom is the Christ according to the flesh, who is over all, God blessed forever. Amen.
NASB.6: But [it is] not as though the word of God has failed. For they are not all Israel who are [descended] from Israel;
NASB.7: nor are they all children because they are Abraham's descendants, but: 'THROUGH ISAAC YOUR DESCENDANTS WILL BE NAMED.'
NASB.8: That is, it is not the children of the flesh who are children of God, but the children of the promise are regarded as descendants.
NASB.9: For this is the word of promise: 'AT THIS TIME I WILL COME, AND SARAH SHALL HAVE A SON.'
NASB.10: And not only this, but there was Rebekah also, when she had conceived [twins] by one man, our father Isaac;
NASB.11: for though [the twins] were not yet born and had not done anything good or bad, so that God's purpose according to [His] choice would stand, not because of works but because of Him who calls,
NASB.12: it was said to her, 'THE OLDER WILL SERVE THE YOUNGER.'
NASB.13: Just as it is written, 'JACOB I LOVED, BUT ESAU I HATED.'
NASB.14: What shall we say then? There is no injustice with God, is there? May it never be!
NASB.15: For He says to Moses, 'I WILL HAVE MERCY ON WHOM I HAVE MERCY, AND I WILL HAVE COMPASSION ON WHOM I HAVE COMPASSION.'
NASB.16: So then it [does] not [depend] on the man who wills or the man who runs, but on God who has mercy.
NASB.17: For the Scripture says to Pharaoh, 'FOR THIS VERY PURPOSE I RAISED YOU UP, TO DEMONSTRATE MY POWER IN YOU, AND THAT MY NAME MIGHT BE PROCLAIMED THROUGHOUT THE WHOLE EARTH.'
NASB.18: So then He has mercy on whom He desires, and He hardens whom He desires.
NASB.19: You will say to me then, 'Why does He still find fault? For who resists His will?'
NASB.20: On the contrary, who are you, O man, who answers back to God? The thing molded will not say to the molder, 'Why did you make me like this,' will it?
NASB.21: Or does not the potter have a right over the clay, to make from the same lump one vessel for honorable use and another for common use?
NASB.22: What if God, although willing to demonstrate His wrath and to make His power known, endured with much patience vessels of wrath prepared for destruction?
NASB.23: And [He did so] to make known the riches of His glory upon vessels of mercy, which He prepared beforehand for glory,
NASB.24: [even] us, whom He also called, not from among Jews only, but also from among Gentiles.
NASB.25: As He says also in Hosea, 'I WILL CALL THOSE WHO WERE NOT MY PEOPLE, 'MY PEOPLE,' AND HER WHO WAS NOT BELOVED, 'BELOVED.''
NASB.26: 'AND IT SHALL BE THAT IN THE PLACE WHERE IT WAS SAID TO THEM, 'YOU ARE NOT MY PEOPLE,' THERE THEY SHALL BE CALLED SONS OF THE LIVING GOD.'
NASB.27: Isaiah cries out concerning Israel, 'THOUGH THE NUMBER OF THE SONS OF ISRAEL BE LIKE THE SAND OF THE SEA, IT IS THE REMNANT THAT WILL BE SAVED;
NASB.28: FOR THE LORD WILL EXECUTE HIS WORD ON THE EARTH, THOROUGHLY AND QUICKLY.'
NASB.29: And just as Isaiah foretold, 'UNLESS THE LORD OF SABAOTH HAD LEFT TO US A POSTERITY, WE WOULD HAVE BECOME LIKE SODOM, AND WOULD HAVE RESEMBLED GOMORRAH.'
NASB.30: What shall we say then? That Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness, attained righteousness, even the righteousness which is by faith;
NASB.31: but Israel, pursuing a law of righteousness, did not arrive at [that] law.
NASB.32: Why? Because [they did] not [pursue it] by faith, but as though [it were] by works. They stumbled over the stumbling stone,
NASB.33: just as it is written, 'BEHOLD, I LAY IN ZION A STONE OF STUMBLING AND A ROCK OF OFFENSE, AND HE WHO BELIEVES IN HIM WILL NOT BE DISAPPOINTED.'

NLT.1: With Christ as my witness, I speak with utter truthfulness. My conscience and the Holy Spirit confirm it.
NLT.2: My heart is filled with bitter sorrow and unending grief
NLT.3: for my people, my Jewish brothers and sisters. I would be willing to be forever cursed — cut off from Christ! — if that would save them.
NLT.4: They are the people of Israel, chosen to be God’s adopted children. God revealed his glory to them. He made covenants with them and gave them his law. He gave them the privilege of worshiping him and receiving his wonderful promises.
NLT.5: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob are their ancestors, and Christ himself was an Israelite as far as his human nature is concerned. And he is God, the one who rules over everything and is worthy of eternal praise! Amen.
NLT.6: Well then, has God failed to fulfill his promise to Israel? No, for not all who are born into the nation of Israel are truly members of God’s people!
NLT.7: Being descendants of Abraham doesn’t make them truly Abraham’s children. For the Scriptures say, “Isaac is the son through whom your descendants will be counted,” though Abraham had other children, too.
NLT.8: This means that Abraham’s physical descendants are not necessarily children of God. Only the children of the promise are considered to be Abraham’s children.
NLT.9: For God had promised, “I will return about this time next year, and Sarah will have a son.”
NLT.10: This son was our ancestor Isaac. When he married Rebekah, she gave birth to twins.
NLT.11: But before they were born, before they had done anything good or bad, she received a message from God. (This message shows that God chooses people according to his own purposes;
NLT.12: he calls people, but not according to their good or bad works.) She was told, “Your older son will serve your younger son.”
NLT.13: In the words of the Scriptures, “I loved Jacob, but I rejected Esau.”
NLT.14: Are we saying, then, that God was unfair? Of course not!
NLT.15:
For God said to Moses, “I will show mercy to anyone I choose,
and I will show compassion to anyone I choose.”
NLT.16: So it is God who decides to show mercy. We can neither choose it nor work for it.
NLT.17: For the Scriptures say that God told Pharaoh, “I have appointed you for the very purpose of displaying my power in you and to spread my fame throughout the earth.”
NLT.18: So you see, God chooses to show mercy to some, and he chooses to harden the hearts of others so they refuse to listen.
NLT.19: Well then, you might say, “Why does God blame people for not responding? Haven’t they simply done what he makes them do?”
NLT.20: No, don’t say that. Who are you, a mere human being, to argue with God? Should the thing that was created say to the one who created it, “Why have you made me like this?”
NLT.21: When a potter makes jars out of clay, doesn’t he have a right to use the same lump of clay to make one jar for decoration and another to throw garbage into?
NLT.22: In the same way, even though God has the right to show his anger and his power, he is very patient with those on whom his anger falls, who are destined for destruction.
NLT.23: He does this to make the riches of his glory shine even brighter on those to whom he shows mercy, who were prepared in advance for glory.
NLT.24: And we are among those whom he selected, both from the Jews and from the Gentiles.
NLT.25:
Concerning the Gentiles, God says in the prophecy of Hosea, “Those who were not my people,
I will now call my people.
And I will love those
whom I did not love before.”
NLT.26:
And, “Then, at the place where they were told,
‘You are not my people,’
there they will be called
‘children of the living God.’”
NLT.27:
And concerning Israel, Isaiah the prophet cried out, “Though the people of Israel are as numerous as the sand of the seashore,
only a remnant will be saved.
NLT.28:
For the LORD will carry out his sentence upon the earth
quickly and with finality.”
NLT.29:
And Isaiah said the same thing in another place: “If the LORD of Heaven’s Armies
had not spared a few of our children,
we would have been wiped out like Sodom,
destroyed like Gomorrah.”
NLT.30: What does all this mean? Even though the Gentiles were not trying to follow God’s standards, they were made right with God. And it was by faith that this took place.
NLT.31: But the people of Israel, who tried so hard to get right with God by keeping the law, never succeeded.
NLT.32: Why not? Because they were trying to get right with God by keeping the law instead of by trusting in him. They stumbled over the great rock in their path.
NLT.33:
God warned them of this in the Scriptures when he said, “I am placing a stone in Jerusalem that makes people stumble,
a rock that makes them fall.
But anyone who trusts in him
will never be disgraced.”

WEB.1: I tell the truth in Christ. I am not lying, my conscience testifying with me in the Holy Spirit,
WEB.2: that I have great sorrow and unceasing pain in my heart.
WEB.3: For I could wish that I myself were accursed from Christ for my brothers’ sake, my relatives according to the flesh,
WEB.4: who are Israelites; whose is the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the giving of the law, the service, and the promises;
WEB.5: of whom are the fathers, and from whom is Christ as concerning the flesh, who is over all, God, blessed forever. Amen.
WEB.6: But it is not as though the word of God has come to nothing. For they are not all Israel, that are of Israel.
WEB.7: Neither, because they are Abraham’s offspring,  are they all children. But, “your offspring  will be accounted as from Isaac.”
WEB.8: That is, it is not the children of the flesh who are children of God, but the children of the promise are counted as heirs.
WEB.9: For this is a word of promise, “At the appointed time I will come, and Sarah will have a son.”
WEB.10: Not only so, but Rebekah also conceived by one, by our father Isaac.
WEB.11: For being not yet born, neither having done anything good or bad, that the purpose of God according to election might stand, not of works, but of him who calls,
WEB.12: it was said to her, “The elder will serve the younger.”
WEB.13: Even as it is written, “Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated.”
WEB.14: What shall we say then? Is there unrighteousness with God? May it never be!
WEB.15: For he said to Moses, “I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion.”
WEB.16: So then it is not of him who wills, nor of him who runs, but of God who has mercy.
WEB.17: For the Scripture says to Pharaoh, “For this very purpose I caused you to be raised up, that I might show in you my power, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth.”
WEB.18: So then, he has mercy on whom he desires, and he hardens whom he desires.
WEB.19: You will say then to me, “Why does he still find fault? For who withstands his will?”
WEB.20: But indeed, O man, who are you to reply against God? Will the thing formed ask him who formed it, “Why did you make me like this?”
WEB.21: Or hasn’t the potter a right over the clay, from the same lump to make one part a vessel for honor, and another for dishonor?
WEB.22: What if God, willing to show his wrath, and to make his power known, endured with much patience vessels of wrath made for destruction,
WEB.23: and that he might make known the riches of his glory on vessels of mercy, which he prepared beforehand for glory,
WEB.24: us, whom he also called, not from the Jews only, but also from the Gentiles?
WEB.25: As he says also in Hosea, “I will call them ‘my people,’ which were not my people; and her ‘beloved,’ who was not beloved.”
WEB.26: “It will be that in the place where it was said to them, ‘You are not my people,’ There they will be called ‘children of the living God.’”
WEB.27: Isaiah cries concerning Israel, “If the number of the children of Israel are as the sand of the sea, it is the remnant who will be saved;
WEB.28: for He will finish the work and cut it short in righteousness, because the LORD will make a short work upon the earth.”
WEB.29: As Isaiah has said before, “Unless the Lord of Armies  had left us a seed, we would have become like Sodom, and would have been made like Gomorrah.”
WEB.30: What shall we say then? That the Gentiles, who didn’t follow after righteousness, attained to righteousness, even the righteousness which is of faith;
WEB.31: but Israel, following after a law of righteousness, didn’t arrive at the law of righteousness.
WEB.32: Why? Because they didn’t seek it by faith, but as it were by works of the law. They stumbled over the stumbling stone;
WEB.33: even as it is written, “Behold,  I lay in Zion a stumbling stone and a rock of offense; and no one who believes in him will be disappointed.”

CSB17.1: I speak the truth in Christ <sup>ⓙ</sup>  — I am not lying; my conscience testifies to me through the Holy Spirit <sup>[1]</sup>  —
CSB17.2: that I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart.
CSB17.3: For I could wish <sup>ⓚ</sup> that I myself were cursed <sup>ⓛ</sup> and cut off <sup>[2]</sup> from Christ for the benefit of my brothers and sisters, my own flesh and blood.
CSB17.4: They are Israelites, <sup>ⓜ</sup> and to them belong the adoption, <sup>ⓝ</sup> the glory, <sup>ⓐ</sup> the covenants, <sup>ⓑ</sup> the giving of the law, <sup>ⓒ</sup> the temple service, <sup>ⓓ</sup> and the promises. <sup>ⓔ</sup>
CSB17.5: The ancestors are theirs, <sup>ⓕ</sup> and from them, by physical descent, <sup>[3]</sup> came the Christ, <sup>ⓖ</sup> who is God <sup>ⓗ</sup> over all, <sup>ⓘ</sup> praised forever. <sup>[4]</sup> <sup>ⓙ</sup> Amen.
CSB17.6: Now it is not as though the word of God has failed, <sup>ⓚ</sup> because not all who are descended from Israel are Israel. <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.7: Neither are all of Abraham’s children his descendants. [5] ⓜ On the contrary, your offspring will be traced [6] through Isaac. [7] ⓝ
CSB17.8: That is, it is not the children by physical descent <sup>[8]</sup> who are God’s children, <sup>ⓐ</sup> but the children of the promise <sup>ⓑ</sup> are considered to be the offspring.
CSB17.9: For this is the statement of the promise: At this time I will come, and Sarah will have a son. [9] ⓒ
CSB17.10: And not only that, <sup>ⓓ</sup> but Rebekah conceived children <sup>ⓔ</sup> through one man, our father Isaac.
CSB17.11: For though her sons had not been born yet or done anything good or bad, so that God’s purpose according to election might stand — <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.12: not from works but from the one who calls — she was told, The older will serve the younger. [10] ⓖ
CSB17.13: As it is written: I have loved Jacob, but I have hated Esau. [11] ⓗ
CSB17.14: What should we say then? <sup>ⓘ</sup> Is there injustice with God? <sup>ⓙ</sup> Absolutely not! <sup>ⓚ</sup>
CSB17.15: For he tells Moses, I will show mercy to whom I will show mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I will have compassion. [12] ⓛ
CSB17.16: So then, it does not depend on human will or effort <sup>ⓜ</sup> but on God who shows mercy. <sup>ⓝ</sup>
CSB17.17: For the Scripture tells Pharaoh, I raised you up for this reason so that I may display my power in you and that my name may be proclaimed in the whole earth. [13] ⓐ
CSB17.18: So then, he has mercy on whom he wants to have mercy and he hardens whom he wants to harden. <sup>ⓑ</sup>
CSB17.19: You will say to me, <sup>ⓒ</sup> therefore, “Why then does he still find fault? <sup>ⓓ</sup> For who can resist his will?” <sup>ⓔ</sup>
CSB17.20: But who are you, a mere man, to talk back to God? <sup>ⓕ</sup> Will what is formed say to the one who formed it, “Why did you make me like this?” <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.21: Or has the potter no right over the clay, <sup>ⓗ</sup> to make from the same lump one piece of pottery for honor and another for dishonor?
CSB17.22: And what if God, wanting to display his wrath and to make his power known, endured with much patience <sup>ⓘ</sup> objects of wrath prepared for destruction? <sup>ⓙ</sup>
CSB17.23: And what if he did this to make known the riches of his glory <sup>ⓚ</sup> on objects of mercy <sup>ⓛ</sup> that he prepared beforehand for glory <sup>ⓜ</sup>  —
CSB17.24: on us, the ones he also called, <sup>ⓝ</sup> not only from the Jews but also from the Gentiles? <sup>ⓐ</sup>
CSB17.25:
As it [14] also says in Hosea, I will call Not My People, My People,
and she who is Unloved, Beloved. [15] ⓑ
CSB17.26:
And it will be in the place where they were told,
you are not my people,
there they will be called sons of the living God. [16] ⓒ
CSB17.27:
But Isaiah cries out concerning Israel, Though the number of Israelites
is like the sand of the sea, ⓓ
only the remnant will be saved; ⓔ
CSB17.28:
since the Lord will execute his sentence
completely and decisively on the earth. [17] [18] ⓕ
CSB17.29:
And just as Isaiah predicted: If the Lord of Hosts [19] had not left us offspring, ⓖ
we would have become like Sodom,
and we would have been made like Gomorrah. [20] ⓗ
CSB17.30: What should we say then? <sup>ⓘ</sup> Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness, have obtained righteousness — namely the righteousness that comes from faith. <sup>ⓙ</sup>
CSB17.31: But Israel, pursuing the law of righteousness, <sup>ⓚ</sup> has not achieved the righteousness of the law. <sup>[21]</sup> <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.32: Why is that? Because they did not pursue it by faith, but as if it were by works. <sup>[22]</sup> They stumbled over the stumbling stone. <sup>ⓜ</sup>
CSB17.33:
As it is written, Look, I am putting a stone in Zion to stumble over
and a rock to trip over,
and the one who believes on him ⓝ
will not be put to shame. [23] ⓐ

AMP.1: I am telling the truth in Christ, I am not lying, my conscience testifies with me [enlightened and prompted] by the Holy Spirit,
AMP.2: that I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart.
AMP.3: For [if it were possible] I would wish that I myself were accursed, [separated, banished] from Christ for the sake [of the salvation] of my brothers, my natural kinsmen,
AMP.4: who are Israelites, to whom belongs the adoption as sons, the glory ( Shekinah), the [special] covenants [with Abraham, Moses, and David], the giving of the Law, the [system of temple] worship, and the [original] promises.
AMP.5: To them belong the patriarchs, and from them, according to His natural descent, came the Christ (the Messiah, the Anointed), He who is exalted and supreme over all, God blessed forever. Amen.
AMP.6: However, it is not as though God's word has failed [coming to nothing]. For not all who are descended from Israel (Jacob) are [the true] Israel;
AMP.7: and they are not all the children of Abraham because they are his descendants [by blood], but [the promise was]: "Your descendants will be named through Isaac" [though Abraham had other sons].
AMP.8: That is, it is not the children of the body [Abraham's natural descendants] who are God's children, but it is the children of the promise who are counted as [Abraham's true] descendants.
AMP.9: For this is what the promise said: "About this time [next year] I will come, and Sarah shall have a son."
AMP.10: And not only that, but this too: Rebekah conceived twin sons by one man [under the same circumstances], by our father Isaac;
AMP.11: and though the twins were not yet born and had not done anything either good or bad, so that God's purpose [His choice, His election] would stand, not because of works [done by either child], but because of [the plan of] Him who calls them,
AMP.12: it was said to her, "<e>The</e><e>older</e> (Esau) <e>will serve the</e><e>younger</e> (Jacob)."
AMP.13: As it is written and forever remains written, "Jacob I loved (chose, protected, blessed), butEsau I hated (held in disregard compared to Jacob)."
AMP.14: What shall we say then? Is there injustice with God? Certainly not!
AMP.15: For He says to Moses, "<e>I will have mercy on whomever I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whomever I have compassion</e>."
AMP.16: So then God's choice is not dependent on human will, nor on human effort [the totality of human striving], but on God who shows mercy [to whomever He chooses—it is His sovereign gift].
AMP.17: For the Scripture says to Pharaoh, "I raised you up for this very purpose, to display My power in [dealing with] you, and so that My name would be proclaimed in all the earth."
AMP.18: So then, He has mercy on whom He wills (chooses), and He hardens [the heart of] whom He wills.
AMP.19: You will say to me then, "Why does He still blame me [for sinning]? For who [including myself] has [ever] resisted His will and purpose?"
AMP.20: On the contrary, who are you, O man, who answers [arrogantly] back to God and dares to defy Him? Will the thing which is formed say to him who formed it, "Why have you made me like this?"
AMP.21: Does the potter not have the right over the clay, to make from the same lump [of clay] one object for honorable use [something beautiful or distinctive] and another for common use [something ordinary or menial]?
AMP.22: What if God, although willing to show His [terrible] wrath and to make His power known, has tolerated with great patience the objects of His wrath [which are] prepared for destruction?
AMP.23: And what if He has done so to make known the riches of His glory to the objects of His mercy, which He has prepared beforehand for glory,
AMP.24: including us, whom He also called, not only from among the Jews, but also from among the Gentiles?
AMP.25:
Just as He says in [the writings of the prophet] Hosea:
"I will call those who were not My people, 'My people,'
And [I will call] her who was not beloved, 'beloved.'"
AMP.26:
"And it shall be that in the place where it was said to them, 'you are not My people,'
There they shall be called sons of the living God."
AMP.27: And Isaiah calls out concerning Israel: "Though the number of the sons of Israel be like the sand of the sea, it is only the remnant [a small believing minority] that will be saved [from God's judgment];
AMP.28: For the Lord will execute His word upon the earth [He will conclude His dealings with mankind] completely and without delay."
AMP.29:
It is as Isaiah foretold,
"If the Lord of Hosts had not left us seed [future generations from which a believing remnant of Israelites came],
We would have become like Sodom, and would have resembled Gomorrah [totally rejected and destroyed]!"
AMP.30: What shall we say then? That Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness [who did not seek salvation and a right relationship with God, nevertheless] obtained righteousness, that is, the righteousness which is produced by faith;
AMP.31: whereas Israel, [though always] pursuing the law of righteousness, did not succeed in fulfilling the law.
AMP.32: And why not? Because it was not by faith [that they pursued it], but as though it were by works [relying on the merit of their works instead of their faith]. They stumbled over the stumbling Stone [Jesus Christ].
AMP.33:
As it is written and forever remains written,
"Behold I am laying in Zion a Stone of stumbling and a Rock of offense;
And he who believes in Him [whoever adheres to, trusts in, and relies on Him] will not be disappointed [in his expectations]."

MSG.1: At the same time, you need to know that I carry with me at all times a huge sorrow.
MSG.2: It's an enormous pain deep within me, and I'm never free of it. I'm not exaggerating—Christ and the Holy Spirit are my witnesses. It's the Israelites...
MSG.3: If there were any way I could be cursed by the Messiah so they could be blessed by him, I'd do it in a minute. They're my family.
MSG.4: I grew up with them. They had everything going for them—family, glory, covenants, revelation, worship, promises,
MSG.5: to say nothing of being the race that produced the Messiah, the Christ, who is God over everything, always. Oh, yes!
MSG.6: Don't suppose for a moment, though, that God's Word has malfunctioned in some way or other. The problem goes back a long way. From the outset, not all Israelites of the flesh were Israelites of the spirit.
MSG.7: It wasn't Abraham's sperm that gave identity here, but God's promise. Remember how it was put: "Your family will be defined by Isaac"?
MSG.8: That means that Israelite identity was never racially determined by sexual transmission, but it was God-determined by promise.
MSG.9: Remember that promise, "When I come back next year at this time, Sarah will have a son"?
MSG.10: And that's not the only time. To Rebecca, also, a promise was made that took priority over genetics. When she became pregnant by our one-of-a-kind ancestor, Isaac,
MSG.11: and her babies were still innocent in the womb—incapable of good or bad—she received a special assurance from God. What God did in this case made it perfectly plain that his purpose is not a hit-or-miss thing dependent on what we do or don't do, but a sure thing determined by his decision, flowing steadily from his initiative.
MSG.12: God told Rebecca, "The firstborn of your twins will take second place."
MSG.13: Later that was turned into a stark epigram: "I loved Jacob; I hated Esau."
MSG.14: Is that grounds for complaining that God is unfair? Not so fast, please.
MSG.15: God told Moses, "I'm in charge of mercy. I'm in charge of compassion."
MSG.16: Compassion doesn't originate in our bleeding hearts or moral sweat, but in God's mercy.
MSG.17: The same point was made when God said to Pharaoh, "I picked you as a bit player in this drama of my salvation power."
MSG.18: All we're saying is that God has the first word, initiating the action in which we play our part for good or ill.
MSG.19: Are you going to object, "So how can God blame us for anything since he's in charge of everything? If the big decisions are already made, what say do we have in it?"
MSG.20: Who in the world do you think you are to second-guess God? Do you for one moment suppose any of us knows enough to call God into question? Clay doesn't talk back to the fingers that mold it, saying, "Why did you shape me like this?"
MSG.21: Isn't it obvious that a potter has a perfect right to shape one lump of clay into a vase for holding flowers and another into a pot for cooking beans?
MSG.22: If God needs one style of pottery especially designed to show his angry displeasure
MSG.23: and another style carefully crafted to show his glorious goodness, isn't that all right?
MSG.24: Either or both happens to Jews, but it also happens to the other people.
MSG.25: Hosea put it well: I'll call nobodies and make them somebodies; I'll call the unloved and make them beloved.
MSG.26: In the place where they yelled out, "You're nobody!" they're calling you "God's living children."
MSG.27: Isaiah maintained this same emphasis: If each grain of sand on the seashore were numbered and the sum labeled "chosen of God," They'd be numbers still, not names; salvation comes by personal selection.
MSG.28: God doesn't count us; he calls us by name. Arithmetic is not his focus.
MSG.29: Isaiah had looked ahead and spoken the truth: If our powerful God had not provided us a legacy of living children, We would have ended up like ghost towns, like Sodom and Gomorrah.
MSG.30: How can we sum this up? All those people who didn't seem interested in what God was doing actually embraced what God was doing as he straightened out their lives.
MSG.31: And Israel, who seemed so interested in reading and talking about what God was doing, missed it.
MSG.32: How could they miss it? Because instead of trusting God, they took over. They were absorbed in what they themselves were doing. They were so absorbed in their "God projects" that they didn't notice God right in front of them, like a huge rock in the middle of the road. And so they stumbled into him and went sprawling.
MSG.33: Isaiah (again!) gives us the metaphor for pulling this together: Careful! I've put a huge stone on the road to Mount Zion, a stone you can't get around. But the stone is me! If you're looking for me, you'll find me on the way, not in the way.

NIV.1:
God’s Sovereign Choice
I speak the truth in Christ—I am not lying, my conscience confirms it in the Holy Spirit—
NIV.2: I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart.
NIV.3: For I could wish that I myself were cursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my brothers, those of my own race,
NIV.4: the people of Israel. Theirs is the adoption as sons; theirs the divine glory, the covenants, the receiving of the law, the temple worship and the promises.
NIV.5: Theirs are the patriarchs, and from them is traced the human ancestry of Christ, who is God over all, forever praised! Amen.
NIV.6: It is not as though God’s word had failed. For not all who are descended from Israel are Israel.
NIV.7: Nor because they are his descendants are they all Abraham’s children. On the contrary, “It is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned.”
NIV.8: In other words, it is not the natural children who are God’s children, but it is the children of the promise who are regarded as Abraham’s offspring.
NIV.9: For this was how the promise was stated: “At the appointed time I will return, and Sarah will have a son.”
NIV.10: Not only that, but Rebekah’s children had one and the same father, our father Isaac.
NIV.11: Yet, before the twins were born or had done anything good or bad—in order that God’s purpose in election might stand:
NIV.12: not by works but by him who calls—she was told, “The older will serve the younger.”
NIV.13: Just as it is written: “Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated.”
NIV.14: What then shall we say? Is God unjust? Not at all!
NIV.15:
For he says to Moses,
“I will have mercy on whom I have mercy,
and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion.”
NIV.16: It does not, therefore, depend on man’s desire or effort, but on God’s mercy.
NIV.17: For the Scripture says to Pharaoh: “I raised you up for this very purpose, that I might display my power in you and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth.”
NIV.18: Therefore God has mercy on whom he wants to have mercy, and he hardens whom he wants to harden.
NIV.19: One of you will say to me: “Then why does God still blame us? For who resists his will?”
NIV.20: But who are you, O man, to talk back to God? “Shall what is formed say to him who formed it, ‘Why did you make me like this?’”
NIV.21: Does not the potter have the right to make out of the same lump of clay some pottery for noble purposes and some for common use?
NIV.22: What if God, choosing to show his wrath and make his power known, bore with great patience the objects of his wrath—prepared for destruction?
NIV.23: What if he did this to make the riches of his glory known to the objects of his mercy, whom he prepared in advance for glory—
NIV.24: even us, whom he also called, not only from the Jews but also from the Gentiles?
NIV.25:
As he says in Hosea:
“I will call them ‘my people’ who are not my people;
and I will call her ‘my loved one’ who is not my loved one,”
NIV.26:
and,
“It will happen that in the very place where it was said to them,
‘You are not my people,’
they will be called ‘sons of the living God.’”
NIV.27:
Isaiah cries out concerning Israel:
“Though the number of the Israelites be like the sand by the sea,
only the remnant will be saved.
NIV.28:
For the Lord will carry out
his sentence on earth with speed and finality.”
NIV.29:
It is just as Isaiah said previously:
“Unless the Lord Almighty
had left us descendants,
we would have become like Sodom,
we would have been like Gomorrah.”
NIV.30:
Israel’s Unbelief
What then shall we say? That the Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness, have obtained it, a righteousness that is by faith;
NIV.31: but Israel, who pursued a law of righteousness, has not attained it.
NIV.32: Why not? Because they pursued it not by faith but as if it were by works. They stumbled over the “stumbling stone.”
NIV.33:
As it is written:
“See, I lay in Zion a stone that causes men to stumble
and a rock that makes them fall,
and the one who trusts in him will never be put to shame.”

NRSVCE.1: I am speaking the truth in Christ — I am not lying; my conscience confirms it by the Holy Spirit —
NRSVCE.2: I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart.
NRSVCE.3: For I could wish that I myself were accursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my own people, my kindred according to the flesh.
NRSVCE.4: They are Israelites, and to them belong the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the giving of the law, the worship, and the promises;
NRSVCE.5: to them belong the patriarchs, and from them, according to the flesh, comes the Messiah, who is over all, God blessed forever. Amen.
NRSVCE.6: It is not as though the word of God had failed. For not all Israelites truly belong to Israel,
NRSVCE.7: and not all of Abraham’s children are his true descendants; but “It is through Isaac that descendants shall be named for you.”
NRSVCE.8: This means that it is not the children of the flesh who are the children of God, but the children of the promise are counted as descendants.
NRSVCE.9: For this is what the promise said, “About this time I will return and Sarah shall have a son.”
NRSVCE.10: Nor is that all; something similar happened to Rebecca when she had conceived children by one husband, our ancestor Isaac.
NRSVCE.11: Even before they had been born or had done anything good or bad (so that God’s purpose of election might continue,
NRSVCE.12: not by works but by his call) she was told, “The elder shall serve the younger.”
NRSVCE.13:
As it is written, “I have loved Jacob,
but I have hated Esau.”
NRSVCE.14: What then are we to say? Is there injustice on God’s part? By no means!
NRSVCE.15:
For he says to Moses, “I will have mercy on whom I have mercy,
and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion.”
NRSVCE.16: So it depends not on human will or exertion, but on God who shows mercy.
NRSVCE.17: For the scripture says to Pharaoh, “I have raised you up for the very purpose of showing my power in you, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth.”
NRSVCE.18: So then he has mercy on whomever he chooses, and he hardens the heart of whomever he chooses.
NRSVCE.19: You will say to me then, “Why then does he still find fault? For who can resist his will?”
NRSVCE.20: But who indeed are you, a human being, to argue with God? Will what is molded say to the one who molds it, “Why have you made me like this?”
NRSVCE.21: Has the potter no right over the clay, to make out of the same lump one object for special use and another for ordinary use?
NRSVCE.22: What if God, desiring to show his wrath and to make known his power, has endured with much patience the objects of wrath that are made for destruction;
NRSVCE.23: and what if he has done so in order to make known the riches of his glory for the objects of mercy, which he has prepared beforehand for glory —
NRSVCE.24: including us whom he has called, not from the Jews only but also from the Gentiles?
NRSVCE.25:
As indeed he says in Hosea, “Those who were not my people I will call ‘my people,’
and her who was not beloved I will call ‘beloved.’”
NRSVCE.26:
“And in the very place where it was said to them, ‘You are not my people,’
there they shall be called children of the living God.”
NRSVCE.27: And Isaiah cries out concerning Israel, “Though the number of the children of Israel were like the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will be saved;
NRSVCE.28: for the Lord will execute his sentence on the earth quickly and decisively.”
NRSVCE.29:
And as Isaiah predicted, “If the Lord of hosts had not left survivors to us,
we would have fared like Sodom
and been made like Gomorrah.”
NRSVCE.30: What then are we to say? Gentiles, who did not strive for righteousness, have attained it, that is, righteousness through faith;
NRSVCE.31: but Israel, who did strive for the righteousness that is based on the law, did not succeed in fulfilling that law.
NRSVCE.32: Why not? Because they did not strive for it on the basis of faith, but as if it were based on works. They have stumbled over the stumbling stone,
NRSVCE.33:
as it is written, “See, I am laying in Zion a stone that will make people stumble, a rock that will make them fall,
and whoever believes in him will not be put to shame.”

NET.1: I am telling the truth in Christ (I am not lying!), for my conscience assures me in the Holy Spirit —
NET.2: I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart.
NET.3: For I could wish that I myself were accursed — cut off from Christ — for the sake of my people, my fellow countrymen,
NET.4: who are Israelites. To them belong the adoption as sons, the glory, the covenants, the giving of the law, the temple worship, and the promises.
NET.5: To them belong the patriarchs, and from them, by human descent, came the Christ, who is God over all, blessed forever! Amen.
NET.6: It is not as though the word of God had failed. For not all those who are descended from Israel are truly Israel,
NET.7: nor are all the children Abraham’s true descendants; rather “through Isaac will your descendants be counted.”
NET.8: This means it is not the children of the flesh who are the children of God; rather, the children of promise are counted as descendants.
NET.9: For this is what the promise declared: “About a year from now I will return and Sarah will have a son.”
NET.10: Not only that, but when Rebekah had conceived children by one man, our ancestor Isaac —
NET.11: even before they were born or had done anything good or bad (so that God’s purpose in election would stand, not by works but by his calling) —
NET.12: it was said to her, “The older will serve the younger,”
NET.13: just as it is written: “Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated.”
NET.14: What shall we say then? Is there injustice with God? Absolutely not!
NET.15: For he says to Moses: “I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion.”
NET.16: So then, it does not depend on human desire or exertion, but on God who shows mercy.
NET.17: For the scripture says to Pharaoh: “For this very purpose I have raised you up, that I may demonstrate my power in you, and that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth.”
NET.18: So then, God has mercy on whom he chooses to have mercy, and he hardens whom he chooses to harden.
NET.19: You will say to me then, “Why does he still find fault? For who has ever resisted his will?”
NET.20: But who indeed are you — a mere human being — to talk back to God? Does what is molded say to the molder, “Why have you made me like this?”
NET.21: Has the potter no right to make from the same lump of clay one vessel for special use and another for ordinary use?
NET.22: But what if God, willing to demonstrate his wrath and to make known his power, has endured with much patience the objects of wrath prepared for destruction?
NET.23: And what if he is willing to make known the wealth of his glory on the objects of mercy that he has prepared beforehand for glory —
NET.24: even us, whom he has called, not only from the Jews but also from the Gentiles?
NET.25: As he also says in Hosea: “I will call those who were not my people, ‘My people,’ and I will call her who was unloved, ‘My beloved.’”
NET.26: “And in the very place where it was said to them, ‘You are not my people,’ there they will be called ‘sons of the living God.’”
NET.27: And Isaiah cries out on behalf of Israel, “Though the number of the children of Israel are as the sand of the sea, only the remnant will be saved,
NET.28: for the Lord will execute his sentence on the earth completely and quickly.”
NET.29: Just as Isaiah predicted, “If the Lord of armies had not left us descendants, we would have become like Sodom, and we would have resembled Gomorrah.”
NET.30: What shall we say then? — that the Gentiles who did not pursue righteousness obtained it, that is, a righteousness that is by faith,
NET.31: but Israel even though pursuing a law of righteousness did not attain it.
NET.32: Why not? Because they pursued it not by faith but (as if it were possible) by works. They stumbled over the stumbling stone,
NET.33: just as it is written, “Look, I am laying in Zion a stone that will cause people to stumble and a rock that will make them fall, yet the one who believes in him will not be put to shame.”
